past_medical_history: HIV diagnosed 2 years ago, on antiretroviral therapy (tenofovir/lamivudine/dolutegravir), most recent CD4 count 68 cells/mm3
Tuberculous meningitis treated for 9 months, completed therapy 3 months ago with full neurological recovery
Pellagra diagnosed during recent hospitalization, treated with niacin supplementation
Chronic malnutrition and wasting syndrome, BMI 17.2
family_history: No known family history of neurological disease

social_history.occupation: Market vendor, currently unemployed due to illness
social_history.living_situation: Lives with extended family in urban setting
social_history.travel: Born and raised in central Africa, no recent travel outside the country

neurological_exam.mental_status: Obtunded. Glasgow Coma Scale 9/15 (E2 V3 M4). Does not open eyes spontaneously; opens briefly to painful stimuli. Vocalizes incomprehensible sounds. Localizes pain with upper extremities. Not oriented to person, place, or time. Unable to follow commands.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive to light. No papilledema on bedside fundoscopy. Corneal reflexes present bilaterally. No facial asymmetry noted. Oculocephalic reflex intact. Gag reflex present.
neurological_exam.motor: Moves all four extremities to painful stimuli. Localizes pain in upper extremities, withdraws lower extremities. No spontaneous purposeful movement observed. Tone mildly increased throughout. No fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws to noxious stimuli symmetrically in all four extremities. Formal sensory testing not possible due to altered consciousness.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 3+ bilaterally, triceps 3+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses extensor bilaterally (bilateral Babinski signs).
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to assess due to altered mental status.
neurological_exam.additional: No nuchal rigidity on examination. No Kernig or Brudzinski signs elicited. Polyserositis with bilateral small pleural effusions and minimal ascites on physical exam. No skin rash or petechiae. Mild generalized muscle wasting. Evidence of chronic malnutrition. No lymphadenopathy. Indwelling urinary catheter in place.

patient.chief_complaint: Acute onset fever and altered consciousness on hospital day 18
patient.history_present_illness: This is a 25-year-old woman with a known history of HIV infection and recently treated tuberculous meningitis who has been hospitalized for the past 18 days for evaluation and management of severe abdominal pain, progressive wasting, and malnutrition. Her antiretroviral therapy consists of tenofovir, lamivudine, and dolutegravir, which was initiated approximately two years ago. Her most recent CD4 count, obtained on admission, was 68 cells per cubic millimeter, and her HIV viral load was 137 copies per milliliter, indicating suboptimal viral suppression. During this hospitalization, serum cryptococcal antigen testing returned positive; however, cerebrospinal fluid cryptococcal antigen was negative, and the patient had no neurological symptoms at that time. She was diagnosed with pellagra and started on niacin supplementation. Her oral antiretroviral medications were temporarily held for several days due to persistent nausea and vomiting, and she was managed with intravenous fluids and nutritional support. On the morning of hospital day 18, nursing staff found the patient febrile to 39 degrees Celsius with acute deterioration in mental status. She had been conversant and oriented the evening prior but was now obtunded, opening her eyes only to painful stimuli and vocalizing incomprehensible sounds. She was not following commands. There were no witnessed seizure-like movements, and no new rash was observed. The patient had an indwelling urinary catheter placed on hospital day 5 for monitoring of urine output. Physical examination revealed signs of polyserositis with bilateral small pleural effusions and minimal ascites, but no accessible fluid collections for diagnostic aspiration. There was no erythema or purulence at catheter insertion sites or peripheral intravenous access sites. Notably, despite the severely altered consciousness, classical meningeal signs including nuchal rigidity, Kernig sign, and Brudzinski sign were absent on examination. The clinical team was concerned for new-onset central nervous system infection versus septic encephalopathy in the setting of severe immunosuppression.

findings.location: Diffuse bilateral cerebral convexities and basal cisterns

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement along bilateral convexities and basal cisterns, most prominent over the frontal and parietal regions
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal within multiple cortical sulci bilaterally, consistent with proteinaceous or inflammatory material in the subarachnoid space
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion within the brain parenchyma; no evidence of acute infarct or abscess
signal_characteristics.T2: Mild hyperintense signal within the sulci

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect or midline shift

signal_characteristics.T1: Mild generalized cerebral volume loss, greater than expected for age, likely related to chronic illness and malnutrition

additional_observations: No parenchymal ring-enhancing lesions to suggest toxoplasmosis or abscess
No basal ganglia or thalamic signal abnormality
Normal appearance of brainstem and cerebellum
No hydrocephalus; ventricles are normal in size and configuration
Small bilateral pleural effusions noted on scout images
mri.impression: No evidence of parenchymal abscess, toxoplasmosis, or other space-occupying lesion. No hydrocephalus. Mild generalized cerebral volume loss greater than expected for age. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Low voltage QRS complexes in limb leads
ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 118 bpm with low voltage QRS complexes in limb leads. No acute ischemic changes. Low voltage may be related to pericardial effusion or malnutrition.

Infection screen.value: WBC >50/hpf, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase, many gram-negative rods

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: White blood cell count 15.7 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 88 % (H), Lymphocytes 6 % (L), Hemoglobin 8.6 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 26.1 % (L), Sodium 119 mmol/L (L), Chloride 88 mmol/L (L), Bicarbonate 18 mmol/L (L), Blood urea nitrogen 28 mg/dL (H), Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL (H), C-reactive protein 187 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 8.4 ng/mL (H), Lactate 3.2 mmol/L (H), AST 62 U/L (H), Total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL (H), Albumin 2.1 g/dL (L), PT 14.8 seconds (H), INR 1.3  (H), D-dimer 4.2 mg/L FEU (H), Serum cryptococcal antigen Positive (titer 1:4)  (H), Urinalysis WBC >50/hpf, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase, many gram-negative rods  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Serum CrAg positive at low titer (1: 4) (L)

special_tests.bacterial_culture: Pending; preliminary identification at 24 hours: Proteus mirabilis

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 28 cmH2O. WBC: N/A (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 312 mg/dL (markedly elevated). Glucose: 12 mg/dL (very low) (ratio 0.13 (CSF/serum, critically low; serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). gram_stain: Gram-negative rods identified on microscopy. CSF_cryptococcal_antigen: Negative. CSF_AFB_stain: Negative. CSF_GeneXpert_MTB_RIF: Not detected. CSF_lactate: 8.4 mmol/L (elevated, consistent with bacterial meningitis).

output.appearance: Slightly hazy, less turbid than initial LP
output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: N/A (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 186 mg/dL (elevated but improving). Glucose: 34 mg/dL (low but improving) (ratio 0.38 (improving from 0.13)). gram_stain: No organisms seen. bacterial_culture: No growth at 48 hours. CSF_lactate: 4.8 mmol/L (still elevated but decreasing).

findings.description: contrast: Diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement on post-contrast CT

output.impression: No acute complications including no hydrocephalus, no subdural empyema, and no evidence of cerebral herniation. Safe to proceed with lumbar puncture. Clinical correlation recommended.

Blood cultures.value: Sensitive to meropenem, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, gentamicin. Resistant to ampicillin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Blood culture set 1 (aerobic) Proteus mirabilis isolated at 14 hours  (H), Blood culture set 2 (aerobic) Proteus mirabilis isolated at 16 hours  (H), Antibiotic susceptibility Sensitive to meropenem, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, gentamicin. Resistant to ampicillin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Blood cultures 2/2 positive for Proteus mirabilis
Sensitive to meropenem, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, gentamicin

results.title: Proteus mirabilis meningitis: a rare cause of neonatal and adult meningitis
results.summary: Case report of Proteus mirabilis meningitis in an HIV-positive adult patient. Highlights the rarity of this pathogen as a cause of meningitis and the association with urinary tract infections and catheter-related bacteremia as potential sources.

results.title: Gram-negative bacillary meningitis in adults: systematic review of 415 cases
results.summary: Gram-negative bacillary meningitis accounts for approximately 5-10% of adult bacterial meningitis cases, most commonly in immunocompromised patients, post-neurosurgical patients, or those with nosocomial infections. E. coli and Klebsiella are most common; Proteus species are rare but documented.

results.title: Management of gram-negative bacterial meningitis
results.summary: Treatment recommendations include third-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone) or meropenem for 21 days minimum for gram-negative bacillary meningitis. Adjunctive dexamethasone benefit is less established for gram-negative meningitis compared to pneumococcal disease.

output.summary: Proteus mirabilis is a rare but recognized cause of bacterial meningitis, most frequently occurring in immunocompromised patients, neonates, or those with urinary tract infections serving as a source of hematogenous dissemination. Treatment requires prolonged antibiotic therapy (minimum 21 days) with agents that achieve adequate CSF penetration, such as ceftriaxone or meropenem. Mortality rates for gram-negative bacillary meningitis remain higher than for community-acquired meningitis due to common pathogens.

output.proposed: Meropenem 2g IV every 8 hours for gram-negative meningitis
interactions: Meropenem + Dolutegravir: No clinically significant interaction. Meropenem does not affect UGT1A1 metabolism of dolutegravir. Both can be administered concurrently.
Meropenem + Tenofovir: Both are renally eliminated. Monitor renal function closely given existing AKI. Dose adjustments may be required if renal function deteriorates.
contraindications: Use with caution in patients with history of seizures; carbapenems lower seizure threshold. However, this risk is outweighed by the need for adequate gram-negative CNS coverage.
warnings: Monitor renal function closely due to concurrent use with tenofovir and pre-existing acute kidney injury
Meropenem may reduce valproic acid levels if coadministered (not currently applicable)
Risk of Clostridioides difficile infection with prolonged carbapenem use
output.formulary_status: Available, restricted to infectious disease approval for extended courses
alternatives: Ceftriaxone 2g IV every 12 hours (if susceptibility confirmed and no ESBL)
Ciprofloxacin 400mg IV every 8 hours (less reliable CNS penetration)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Proteus mirabilis

differential.key_distinguishing: Patient completed 9 months of TB treatment with full neurological recovery. CSF GeneXpert negative. CSF profile shows acute neutrophilic predominance rather than lymphocytic, and protein/glucose ratio is more extreme than typical TB meningitis.
Despite positive serum CrAg, CSF cryptococcal antigen is negative. CSF profile shows neutrophilic predominance rather than lymphocytic, which is atypical for cryptococcal meningitis.
CSF shows neutrophilic pleocytosis with very low glucose and high protein, inconsistent with viral etiology. PCR panel negative for HSV and enterovirus. No temporal lobe abnormalities on MRI.
No focal neurological deficits. MRI shows no ring-enhancing parenchymal lesions. Presentation is more consistent with diffuse meningeal process.

Septic encephalopathy was the initial working diagnosis due to absent meningeal signs, but CSF analysis reveals clear bacterial meningitis with high cell count, low glucose, and gram-negative organisms on stain.

optimal_actions.action: Assess hemodynamic stability and initiate resuscitation with IV fluids and vasopressors if needed
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hypotension (92/58) and tachycardia (118) requiring fluid resuscitation

optimal_actions.action: Order CBC, BMP, inflammatory markers (CRP, procalcitonin), blood cultures, and infection screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with neutrophilia, severe hyponatremia, markedly elevated CRP and procalcitonin, UTI on urinalysis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head prior to lumbar puncture given altered mental status
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leptomeningeal enhancement, no contraindication to LP

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, CrAg, and TB testing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Turbid CSF, elevated opening pressure, neutrophilic pleocytosis (2840 cells), elevated protein (312), very low glucose (12), gram-negative rods on stain

optimal_actions.action: Initiate empiric IV antibiotics immediately (do not delay for LP) - ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h plus ampicillin for Listeria coverage given immunosuppression
Consider adjunctive dexamethasone (though benefit less established for gram-negative meningitis)
Review blood culture and CSF culture results; narrow antibiotic therapy based on susceptibility
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Proteus mirabilis in blood and CSF cultures; sensitive to ceftriaxone and meropenem

optimal_actions.action: Remove or replace indwelling urinary catheter as likely source of bacteremia
Check drug interactions between meningitis antibiotics and antiretroviral therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No major interaction between meropenem/ceftriaxone and TLD regimen; monitor renal function with tenofovir

optimal_actions.action: Correct severe hyponatremia cautiously (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome)
Repeat LP at 48 hours to assess treatment response
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improving CSF parameters with decreasing pleocytosis and improving glucose

optimal_actions.action: Assess for hearing loss as a complication of bacterial meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Possible sensorineural hearing loss on audiometry

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture with full CSF analysis (cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture)
Initiate empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately, do not delay for diagnostic workup
Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Order brain imaging (CT or MRI) before LP given altered mental status and immunosuppression
Correct severe hyponatremia cautiously to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome
Remove indwelling urinary catheter as likely source of gram-negative bacteremia
Resume antiretroviral therapy as soon as clinically feasible
contraindicated_actions: Delay antibiotics pending diagnostic workup results
Perform LP without prior neuroimaging in a patient with altered mental status and immunosuppression
Rapidly correct hyponatremia (risk of central pontine myelinolysis/osmotic demyelination syndrome)
Administer nephrotoxic agents without renal dose adjustment given acute kidney injury
Discontinue antiretroviral therapy long-term without infectious disease consultation
Assume septic encephalopathy without performing lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis
key_reasoning_points: Absent meningeal signs (nuchal rigidity, Kernig, Brudzinski) do not exclude bacterial meningitis, especially in immunocompromised patients -- LP must still be performed
Positive serum cryptococcal antigen with negative CSF CrAg does not indicate CNS cryptococcal disease; the CSF profile is inconsistent with fungal meningitis
History of treated TB meningitis does not preclude new bacterial infection from a different organism; CSF GeneXpert negative argues against TB recurrence
Gram-negative rod meningitis in an adult with an indwelling catheter and UTI suggests hematogenous spread from a urogenital source (Proteus mirabilis)
Proteus mirabilis is a rare cause of meningitis in adults but is well-documented in immunocompromised patients with urinary tract infections
Severe hyponatremia (Na 119) in the setting of meningitis may be due to SIADH or cerebral salt wasting; correction must be gradual (no more than 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours)
The differential must systematically exclude opportunistic infections (toxoplasmosis, cryptococcal, TB) given severe immunosuppression (CD4 68)
RPR negative and malaria rapid test negative help narrow the differential in this geographic and clinical context

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic CSF findings of bacterial meningitis (high neutrophilic pleocytosis, very low glucose, elevated protein, gram-negative rods on stain) and supporting systemic markers of severe bacterial infection. The atypical feature of absent meningeal signs is offset by clearly diagnostic CSF and imaging findings.
